name: Saudi Arabia

visa_free.nationality: GCC nationals (Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, United Arab Emirates)
visa_free.notes: Citizens of all GCC member states enjoy visa-free entry to Saudi Arabia using their passports under GCC freedom-of-movement agreements. Saudi Arabia is in the process of permitting movement by national ID card.

Mutual visa exemption agreement entered into force May 11, 2026. Covers all passport types including ordinary. 90 days per visit or in multiple periods within a calendar year. Does not apply to work, study, residency, or Hajj. Russia is the first country with which the Kingdom has concluded a mutual visa exemption agreement covering ordinary passports.

e_visa.notes: 1-year multiple-entry e-visa. 90 days maximum stay per year. SAR 480 (approx. USD 128) including insurance.

visa_policy.notes: Saudi Arabia offers a tourist e-visa (SAR 480 including insurance, 1-year multiple-entry, 90 days max stay per year) to citizens of 66 countries as of May 2024. The program was launched in September 2019 with 49 countries and has since been expanded. Uzbekistan and Tajikistan were added in August 2023 but removed from the eligible list in April 2025; they are excluded here. Russia was previously on the e-visa list but is now fully visa-free under a bilateral mutual exemption agreement in force since May 11, 2026 (covers all passport types including ordinary), reducing the current e-visa list to approximately 64 countries. Holders of valid US, UK, or Schengen visas (used at least once) are also eligible via conditional access. GCC nationals (Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, UAE) enjoy visa-free entry under GCC freedom of movement. The total confirmed by SPA as of May 2024 was 66 countries; effective count changes with Russia's upgrade to visa-free and Uzbekistan/Tajikistan removal.

visa_types.name: Tourist e-Visa
visa_types.notes: Available to nationals of 50+ eligible countries. Multiple-entry, up to 90 days per visit within 1-year validity. Applied online at visitsaudi.com or through Absher platform. Mandatory travel insurance included in fee. Alcohol prohibited; dress code modesty rules apply.

visa_types.name: Visa on Arrival
visa_types.notes: Available to nationals of eligible countries at Saudi international airports. Same fee and conditions as the e-Visa (mandatory travel insurance included). Available to same 50+ nationalities eligible for e-Visa. Effectively the same product issued on arrival rather than pre-travel.

visa_types.name: Work Visa / Iqama (Residence Permit)
visa_types.notes: Employer-sponsored. Work visa issued by Saudi embassy abroad; upon arrival, converted to Iqama (residence permit) within 90 days. Iqama valid 1-2 years, renewable. Kafala (sponsorship) system - employer is legal sponsor. Annual Iqama fees: SAR 2,400-4,800 depending on category. Fee listed is approximate combined cost. Employees under kafala require employer permission to change jobs (partially reformed via Saudi Vision 2030 labor reforms).

visa_types.name: Premium Residency (Saudi Green Card)
visa_types.notes: Two tracks: (1) Permanent Premium Residency: SAR 800,000 (approx. USD 213,000) one-time fee, lifetime residence without sponsor; (2) Temporary Premium Residency: SAR 100,000/year (approx. USD 26,667/year), annual renewable. No kafala sponsor required. Holders may work, invest, own property, sponsor family. Applied via pr.gov.sa. Available to select eligible foreigners. Fee shown is for permanent track.

conditional_access.conditions: Holds a valid Tourist or Business Visa from the United States of America. Visa shall have been used at least once to enter the country granting the visa. Includes the visa holder's first-degree relatives travelling with them. 1-year multiple-entry tourist visa; fee SAR 300 per Tourist Visa Regulations Article 9 (SAR 480 including mandatory insurance per practice). Passport valid ≥6 months required.

Holds a valid Tourist or Business Visa from the United Kingdom. Visa shall have been used at least once to enter the country granting the visa. Includes the visa holder's first-degree relatives travelling with them. 1-year multiple-entry tourist visa; fee SAR 300 per Tourist Visa Regulations Article 9 (SAR 480 including mandatory insurance per practice). Passport valid ≥6 months required.

Holds a valid Tourist or Business Visa from one of the countries of the Schengen Agreement. Visa shall have been used at least once to enter the country granting the visa. Includes the visa holder's first-degree relatives travelling with them. 1-year multiple-entry tourist visa; fee SAR 300 per Tourist Visa Regulations Article 9 (SAR 480 including mandatory insurance per practice). Passport valid ≥6 months required.

Has obtained a Permanent Residence in the United States of America. Includes the Residence holder's first-degree relatives travelling with them. 1-year multiple-entry tourist visa; fee SAR 300 per Tourist Visa Regulations Article 9 (SAR 480 including mandatory insurance per practice). Passport valid ≥6 months required.

Has obtained a Permanent Residence in the European Union (any EU country). Includes the Residence holder's first-degree relatives travelling with them. 1-year multiple-entry tourist visa; fee SAR 300 per Tourist Visa Regulations Article 9 (SAR 480 including mandatory insurance per practice). Passport valid ≥6 months required.

Has obtained a Permanent Residence in the United Kingdom. Includes the Residence holder's first-degree relatives travelling with them. 1-year multiple-entry tourist visa; fee SAR 300 per Tourist Visa Regulations Article 9 (SAR 480 including mandatory insurance per practice). Passport valid ≥6 months required.

credential.subtype: Residence Visa valid ≥3 months (eligible professions only)

conditional_access.conditions: Has obtained a Residence Visa valid for a period of no less than three months in the GCC countries, for the professions published on www.visitsaudi.com. Includes the Residence Visa holder's first-degree relatives travelling with them, and workers in domestic professions travelling with their sponsors. 1-year multiple-entry tourist visa; fee SAR 300 per Tourist Visa Regulations Article 9 (SAR 480 including mandatory insurance per practice). Passport valid ≥6 months required.

Agreement between the Government of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and the Government of the Republic of India providing for the mutual exemption from short-stay visa requirements for holders of diplomatic, special, and official passports of both countries. Signed December 17, 2025 in Riyadh.

Agreement between the governments of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and the Republic of Türkiye on mutual exemption from visa requirements for holders of diplomatic and special passports. Signed May 6, 2026 in Ankara. Ordinary passport holders remain eligible for the tourist e-visa.

Agreement between the Government of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and the Government of the Arab Republic of Egypt on mutual exemption from short-stay visa requirements for holders of diplomatic, special, and service passports. Signed March 19, 2026 in Riyadh by Prince Faisal bin Farhan (Saudi FM) and Badr Abdelatty (Egyptian FM). Entry into force date not confirmed; not applicable to ordinary passport holders.

Agreement between the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and the Kingdom of Spain on mutual visa exemption for holders of diplomatic, special, and service passports. Signed May 15, 2026 in Madrid during Saudi FM Prince Faisal bin Farhan's visit. Does not apply to ordinary passport holders; Spanish ordinary passport holders remain eligible for the tourist e-visa. Entry into force date not confirmed in official sources.

cbi.notes: Saudi Arabia does not offer a formal Citizenship-by-Investment (CBI) program. Saudi citizenship is governed by the Saudi Arabian Nationality System under the Ministry of Interior. Naturalization is possible but subject to strict discretionary criteria. Saudi Arabia does occasionally grant citizenship to exceptional talents at the King's discretion (e.g., prominent researchers, innovators, and athletes), but this is not a structured investment-for-citizenship program. No official CBI program was found on any official government domain.

programs.name: Premium Residency - Limited Duration (Temporary)
programs.notes: Annual fee of SAR 100,000 per year, renewable for up to 5 years. Does not automatically lead to permanent residency or citizenship. Administered by the Saudi Premium Residency Center.

programs.name: Premium Residency - Unlimited Duration (Permanent)
programs.notes: One-time fee of SAR 800,000 for permanent residency. No path to citizenship confirmed officially. Holders do not require a local sponsor. Benefits include family sponsorship, ability to conduct business, own real estate, and exemption from expatriate fees.

programs.name: Investor Premium Residency
programs.notes: Requires a minimum investment of SAR 7 million in Saudi Arabia, a valid investment license, and creation of at least 10 jobs within 2 years. Grants conditional permanent residency. Administered by Premium Residency Center.

programs.name: Entrepreneur Premium Residency
programs.notes: Two tracks: (1) Investment of SAR 400,000 with at least 20% ownership stake; (2) Investment of SAR 15 million with at least 10% ownership stake and creation of 20 jobs within 2 years. Benefits include nominating 2 staff members for Special Talent Residency and exemption from Nitaqat workforce localization for 3 years.

programs.name: Real Estate Owner Premium Residency
programs.notes: Requires ownership of Saudi real estate worth a minimum of SAR 4 million. Residency duration is tied to property ownership. New laws effective January 2026 bolstered this category.

programs.name: Special Talent Premium Residency
programs.notes: Targets exceptional professionals in scientific, healthcare, research, and executive fields. Salary thresholds range from SAR 14,000 to SAR 80,000 per month depending on designation. Application fee SAR 4,000. Points-based eligibility system.

programs.name: Gifted Premium Residency
programs.notes: For exceptional talents in sports, culture, and arts. Requires national or international recognition, prestigious award, or official recognition with endorsement from relevant Saudi ministry. Application fee SAR 4,000. Granted for 5 years, renewable once for another 5 years (minimum 30 months residence required for renewal).

programs.name: Special Talent Residency (Premium Residency)
programs.eligibility: Exceptional professionals in scientific, healthcare, research, and executive leadership fields. Points-based system; salary thresholds from SAR 14,000–80,000/month depending on specialization. Requires endorsement and demonstrated exceptional expertise.
programs.notes: In October 2024, Saudi Arabia's Health Minister granted Premium Residency to 2,645 exceptional healthcare professionals from 56 countries under this program. Application fee SAR 4,000.

programs.name: CODE (Coding Institute for Digital Excellence) Premium Residency - MCIT
programs.eligibility: Tech professionals and digital talent invited or sponsored through the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology's CODE program.
programs.notes: The Ministry of Communications and Information Technology (MCIT) offers a premium residency pathway for tech talent through the CODE institute. Specific eligibility criteria are detailed on the MCIT/CODE official pages.

programs.name: Regional Headquarters (RHQ) Program
programs.eligibility: Multinational companies establishing regional headquarters in Saudi Arabia. Eligible employees and executives may obtain facilitated residency.
programs.notes: Administered by the Ministry of Investment (MISA). Companies relocating or establishing RHQ in Saudi Arabia and their qualifying employees benefit from expedited residency and work permit processes.

agreements: GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council) - freedom of movement for GCC nationals
Saudi Arabia–India - mutual visa exemption for diplomatic, special, and official passport holders (signed December 17, 2025)
Saudi Arabia–Türkiye - mutual visa exemption for diplomatic and special passport holders (signed May 6, 2026)
Saudi Arabia–Russia - mutual visa exemption for all passport types including ordinary (signed December 1, 2025; in force May 11, 2026)
Saudi Arabia–Egypt - mutual visa exemption for diplomatic, special, and service passport holders (signed March 19, 2026)
Saudi Arabia–Spain - mutual visa exemption for diplomatic, special, and service passport holders (signed May 15, 2026)

validation_notes: CORRECTION: Russia (RUS) moved from visa_policy.e_visa to visa_policy.visa_free. The mutual visa exemption agreement entered into force May 11, 2026 and covers all passport types including ordinary passports. Confirmed from official SPA article https://www.spa.gov.sa/en/N2582764 fetched directly on 2026-06-13.

CORRECTION: Saudi Arabia–Russia agreement notes clarified: SPA confirms 'Russia is the first country with which the Kingdom has concluded a mutual visa exemption agreement for holders of ordinary passports.' The prior record placed Russia in e_visa - this was stale data as of the in-force date (May 11, 2026).

CONFIRMED: Saudi Arabia–Türkiye diplomatic/special passport agreement (signed May 6, 2026) confirmed from SPA article https://spa.gov.sa/en/N2579352 fetched directly. No ordinary passport visa-free for Turkey - ordinary passport remains e-visa.
CONFIRMED: Saudi Arabia–India diplomatic/special/official passport agreement (signed December 17, 2025) confirmed from SPA article https://www.spa.gov.sa/en/N2470556 fetched directly.
CONFIRMED: e-visa expansion to 66 countries (May 2024) confirmed from SPA article https://www.spa.gov.sa/en/N2098278 fetched directly. Bahamas, Barbados, Grenada correctly listed.
NOTE: Uzbekistan and Tajikistan were previously in the e-visa list (added August 2023) but were removed in April 2025. They are correctly absent from the e_visa entries and noted in the policy notes.
NOTE: e_visa expansion notes for Bahamas, Barbados, Grenada changed from '2024 expansion' to 'May 2024 expansion' to match the SPA article date (May 8, 2024).
NOTE: Turkey e_visa entry updated to clarify distinction between ordinary passport (e-visa) and diplomatic/special passport (visa-free under May 2026 agreement).

ADDITION (2026-06-27): Saudi Arabia–Egypt mutual visa exemption agreement for diplomatic, special, and service passport holders added to conditional_access and agreements. Signed March 19, 2026 in Riyadh. Confirmed via Arab News report (arabnews.com/node/2636964/amp) and SPA article index entry https://www.spa.gov.sa/en/N2541609. SPA article returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch; agreement details confirmed from Arab News (official source corroboration via MOFA/SPA search results). Egypt ordinary passport holders remain subject to standard visa requirements. No visa_policy (ordinary passport) change warranted.

CONFIRMED (2026-06-27): Saudi Arabia–Russia visa exemption confirmed again from MOFA official page - signed December 1, 2025, covers 'all types of passports (diplomatic, special and regular)', up to 90 consecutive or nonconsecutive days within a year. MOFA source: https://www.mofa.gov.sa/en/ministry/news/Pages/...

ADDITION (2026-06-29): Saudi Arabia–Spain mutual visa exemption agreement for diplomatic, special, and service passport holders added to conditional_access and agreements. Signed May 15, 2026 in Madrid. Source: SPA article https://spa.gov.sa/en/N2585110 (returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch; confirmed from WebSearch and secondary source corroboration at visahq.com/news/2026-05-15 identifying diplomatic/special/service passports only). Spanish ordinary passport holders remain on e-visa list - no ordinary-passport access change warranted. Spain entry in e_visa list updated with a note referencing the diplomatic-level agreement.

CONFIRMED (2026-06-29): Russia (visa_free, ordinary passport) - re-confirmed via MOFA official page (mofa.gov.sa) which loaded and described the agreement as covering 'all types of passports (diplomatic, special and regular)', 90 days. No change needed.

CONFIRMED (2026-06-29): No new ordinary passport visa-free agreements found beyond Russia. Russia remains the only country with an ordinary-passport mutual visa exemption with Saudi Arabia as of June 29, 2026.
gaps: The official visa.visitsaudi.com portal returns HTTP 403, preventing direct extraction of the comprehensive, definitive list of currently eligible e-visa countries.

Uzbekistan and Tajikistan removal in April 2025: referenced in secondary sources but no official Saudi government press release confirming the removal was found; they are excluded based on credible reports.
Exact entry-into-force date for the Saudi Arabia–India diplomatic passport agreement is not stated in the SPA announcement (only signing date December 17, 2025 confirmed).
Exact entry-into-force date and max stay days for the Saudi Arabia–Türkiye diplomatic/special passport agreement not stated in the SPA announcement (only signing date May 6, 2026 confirmed).

Exact entry-into-force date and max stay days for the Saudi Arabia–Egypt diplomatic/special/service passport agreement not confirmed (only signing date March 19, 2026 confirmed; SPA article https://www.spa.gov.sa/en/N2541609 returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch).

Exact entry-into-force date and max stay days for the Saudi Arabia–Spain diplomatic/special/service passport agreement not confirmed (only signing date May 15, 2026 confirmed; SPA article https://spa.gov.sa/en/N2585110 returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch).

Precise effective count of the e-visa eligible countries as of June 2026 is uncertain: started at 66 (May 2024), then Uzbekistan/Tajikistan removed (−2 = 64), and Russia moved to fully visa-free; the official portal cannot be scraped to verify.
Exact processing times for Premium Residency categories not found on official pages (pr.gov.sa is JavaScript-rendered).
GCC resident eligible professions list (Article 6.2.d of Tourist Visa Regulations) could not be extracted due to 403 access errors on visitsaudi.com.
Additional bilateral diplomatic/service passport waiver agreements beyond India, Turkey, Russia, Egypt, and Spain may exist but were not identified on official sources during this session.
OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) cardholder status: No official Saudi government source specifically grants entry benefits to OCI cardholders. Not recorded.

outbound_official.notes: Saudi Arabia's Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MOFA) has a page titled 'Travel Facilities for Saudi Citizens for Visiting Purposes' (mofa.gov.sa/en/embassies/TravelInfo/Pages/default.aspx) indexed in search engines as the official Saudi government outbound travel page. However, this page consistently returns a 404 'requested page is unavailable' error on both English and Arabic URLs and cannot be accessed or read. No other official Saudi government domain (Jawazat/General Directorate of Passports, MOI, my.gov.sa, spa.gov.sa) publishes a comprehensive outbound visa-free country list for Saudi ordinary passport holders. Saudi Arabia has not published a retrievable official outbound visa matrix analogous to India's mea.gov.in/vffin. Rely on destination-side data and bilateral agreement announcements (spa.gov.sa) for Saudi outbound access.
